papers.title: Constraints on the Galactic Centre environment from \\\\textit{Gaia} hypervelocity stars III: Insights on a possible companion to Sgr A*
papers.author_names: Fraser A. Evans Alexander Rasskazov Amber Remmelzwaal Tommaso Marchetti Alfred Castro-Ginard Elena Maria Rossi Jo Bovy

abstract.text: We consider a scenario in which Sgr A* is in a massive black hole binary (MBHB) with an as-of-yet undetected supermassive or intermediate-mass black hole companion. Dynamical encounters between this MBHB and single stars in its immediate vicinity would eject hypervelocity stars (HVSs) with velocities beyond the Galactic escape velocity. In this work, we use existing HVS observations to constrain for the first time the existence of a companion to Sgr A*. We simulate the ejection of HVSs via the `MBHB slingshot' scenario and show that the population of HVSs detectable today depends strongly on the companion mass and the separation of the MBHB. We demonstrate that the lack of uncontroversial HVS candidates in \\\\textit{Gaia} Data Release 3 places a firm upper limit on the mass of a possible Sgr A* companion. Within one milliparsec of Sgr A*, our results exclude a companion more massive than $1000 \\\\, \\\\mathrm{M_\\\\odot}$. If Sgr A* recently merged with a companion black hole, our findings indicate that unless this companion was less massive than $500 \\\\, \\\\mathrm{M_\\\\odot}$, this merger must have occurred at least $10$ Myr ago. These results complement and improve upon existing independent constraints on a companion to Sgr A* and show that large regions of its parameter space can now be ruled out.

brief.tldr: [research paper] This study uses Gaia Data Release 3 hypervelocity star (HVS) observations to constrain the existence of a massive black hole companion to Sgr A* via the 'MBHB slingshot' mechanism. By simulating HVS ejection dynamics in a massive black hole binary, the authors show that the absence of detectable HVSs places a strict upper limit of 1000 M⊙ on the companion mass within 1 milliparsec of Sgr A*, and implies that any past merger must have occurred at least 10 Myr ago if the companion exceeded 500 M⊙, thereby ruling out large regions of the companion's parameter space.

source_fields.title: Constraints on the Galactic Centre environment from \\textit{Gaia} hypervelocity stars III: Insights on a possible companion to Sgr A*

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Results: The experiment finds that Gaia DR3 and DR4 are expected to detect 1 to tens of thousands of hypervelocity stars (HVSs) depending on the companion mass (with a minimum of ~800 M⊙ for detectable HVSs) and current separation (with a plateau below the hardening separation), with the population strongly rising for near-equal-mass binaries and steeply increasing with companion mass and decreasing separation. Discussion: Hills-mechanism-ejected HVSs are, on average, slightly more massive and younger than MBHB slingshot-ejected ones, with subtle differences in kinematics and detectability that become more pronounced under specific conditions of the Sgr A* companion mass and separation. Conclusions: The absence of detectable hypervelocity stars in Gaia DR3 strongly constrains the existence of a massive companion or former companion to Sgr A*, with only very low-mass or distant MBHB configurations remaining viable. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel method to constrain the presence of a hidden supermassive or intermediate-mass black hole companion to Sgr A* by analyzing the expected population and velocities of hypervelocity stars ejected through dynamical interactions, thereby solving the long-standing problem of detecting such a companion in the challenging Galactic Centre environment. Massive black hole binary Slingshot Ejection Model: The paper proposes a model that simulates the orbital decay of a massive black hole binary (MBHB) in the Galactic Center through both stellar ejections and gravitational wave emission, tracks the resulting hypervelocity stars (HVSs) as they propagate through the Galaxy, and generates mock Gaia observations to assess detectability, extending prior work with improved physical prescriptions and a broader range of MBHB configurations.

abstract: Dynamical encounters between this MBHB and single stars in its immediate vicinity would eject hypervelocity stars (HVSs) with velocities beyond the Galactic escape velocity.
title: Constraints on the Galactic Centre environment from \\\\textit{Gaia} hypervelocity stars III: Insights

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper uses existing Gaia DR3 HVS observations to constrain theoretical MBHB scenario, but does not present new object-level observational data. Focus is on simulations and dynamical constraints.

papers.title: High-velocity CP2 stars in the Galactic halo
papers.author_names: N. Faltová M. Prišegen K. Bernhard S. Hümmerich E. Paunzen

abstract.text: Context. The second subclass of chemically peculiar stars, the CP2 stars, are early-type stars exhibiting anomalous abundances with variable line strengths possibly also accompanied by photometric variability that typically belong to the Galactic disk. However, a small fraction of these objects were recently found to be located far from the Galactic plane and are thought to belong to the Galactic halo, which is unexpected for this class of objects. Aims. Our study investigates the halo membership of the proposed halo CP2 star candidates based on their velocities and Galactic orbits, to determine their points of origin. In addition, we searched for further halo CP2 star candidates using an as-yet-unpublished sample of CP stars. Methods. Our analysis relied on the astrometry from the early third \\\\textit{Gaia} data release and radial velocities based on the spectroscopy from LAMOST and SDSS, which was also employed in spectral classification. The light variability of the confirmed CP2 stars in our sample was analyzed using data from the ZTF and ATLAS surveys. Results. After filtering our initial sample using kinematic and spectroscopic criteria, we identified six CP2 stars with kinematical properties consistent with a halo membership. The orbits of these stars are in agreement with an origin in the Galactic disk where they were probably ejected through dynamical interactions or in the binary supernova scenario, making them the first runaway CP2 stars known.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: high-velocity or extreme-velocity stars

brief.tldr: [astronomical research paper] The study identifies six CP2 stars with kinematic properties consistent with Galactic halo membership, revealing they likely originated in the disk and were ejected via dynamical interactions or binary supernova events—making them the first known runaway CP2 stars. Analysis combines Gaia EDR3 astrometry, LAMOST/SDSS radial velocities, and photometric variability data to confirm halo origins and challenge the conventional expectation that CP2 stars reside exclusively in the Galactic disk.

source_fields.title: High-velocity CP2 stars in the Galactic halo

source_fields.sections_text: Data: The study of CP star kinematics relies on Gaia's 5D astrometric data for position and proper motion, supplemented by radial velocity measurements from ground-based surveys like LAMOST and SDSS to complete the 6D phase space. Introduction: This study presents the first detailed investigation of two halo magnetic chemically peculiar (mCP) stars, addressing the problem of how such high-velocity, distant stars—unexpected in the context of short-lived B/A-type stars—can exist, thereby challenging models of stellar evolution and magnetic braking in the Galactic halo. Photometric variability: The photometric analysis of six Galactic halo CP2 stars using ZTF and ATLAS data confirms five as amplitude- and period-consistent anti-phase or single-band variable stars, consistent with their expected atmospheric chemical peculiarities, while one shows no detectable variability. Spectral classification: Spectral classification of high-velocity CP2 stars in the Galactic halo using the refined MK system reveals that six bona fide CP2 stars exhibit distinct metallic-line peculiarities, particularly enhanced Eu II features, suggesting a possible link to their unusual kinematics. Summary and conclusions: We identified six CP2 stars with halo kinematics and orbits consistent with being runaway stars ejected from the Galactic disk, marking the first known runaway CP2 stars and demonstrating the power of Gaia astrometry combined with spectroscopic data for studying Galactic halo populations. Identifying halo candidates: Halo candidates are identified kinematically using Gaia EDR3 astrometry, LAMOST, and SDSS radial velocities, with parallax and proper motion biases corrected and stars selected based on low parallax error and full 6D phase space constraints for origin and ejection velocity analysis. Halo membership and Galactic orbits: The halo CP2 stars exhibit kinematics distinct from the Galactic disk, residing in halo-like regions of the Toomre diagram and showing a low probability of belonging to the thick disk, indicating their likely halo membership. Appendix B Galactic orbits of the halo CP stars: This section presents the Galactic orbits of additional halo CP stars using Gaia DR3 data, illustrated through 3D projection plots similar to those in Figure 4. Appendix A Phased light curves and Fourier spectra of the halo CP2 stars: This section presents phased light curves and Fourier spectra of five photometrically variable halo CP2 stars, using which rotational periods are determined from the dominant peaks in the spectra and confirmed through the light curve phase folding.

title: High-velocity CP2 stars in the Galactic halo

catalog_assessment.evidence: Identifies six specific runaway CP2 stars using Gaia EDR3 astrometry, LAMOST/SDSS radial velocities, and photometry. Presents candidate list with kinematic analysis and origin investigation.

papers.title: A runaway T-Tauri star leaving an extended trail
papers.author_names: Josep Martí Pedro L. Luque-Escamilla Estrella Sánchez-Ayaso

abstract.text: Aims. We address the problem of young stellar objects that are found too far away from possible star formation sites. Different mechanisms have been proposed before to explain this unexpected circumstance. The idea of high-velocity protostars is one of these mechanisms, although observational support is not always easy to obtain. We aim to shed light on this issue after the serendipitous discovery of a related stellar system. Methods. Following the inspection of archival infrared data, a peculiar anonymous star was found that apparently heads a long tail that resembles a wake-like feature. We conducted a multiwavelength analysis including photometry, astrometry, and spectroscopy. Together with theoretical physical considerations, this approach provided a reasonable knowledge of the stellar age and kinematic properties, together with compelling indications that the extended feature is indeed the signature of a high-velocity, or runaway, newborn star. Results. Our main result is the discovery of a low-mass young stellar object that fits the concept of a runaway T-Tauri star that was hypothesized several decades ago. In this peculiar star, nicknamed UJT-1, the interaction of the stellar wind with the surrounding medium becomes extreme. Under reasonable assumptions, this unusual degree of interaction has the potential to encode the mass-loss history of the star on timescales of several $10^5$ years

triage.label: rule-weak-llm-confirmed
triage.reason: Weak title match: runaway-related mechanisms LLM confirmed: Explicitly about runaway T-Tauri star, high-velocity protostar with wake feature

brief.skipped_reason: weak rule match; retained with search metadata only

source_fields.sections_text: Discussion: UJT-1 is identified as a bona fide runaway T-Tauri star (RATTS) with TTS-like spectral and photometric properties, a wake extending over 50 pc, and a crossing lifetime of ~1 Myr consistent with its age, demonstrating significant long-range impacts on the interstellar medium comparable to relativistic jets. Conclusions: UJT-1 is confirmed as a supersonic TTS with a well-defined bow shock, providing insights into stellar-ISM interactions and offering a potential window into the mass-loss history of young stars, while speculative interpretations suggest a nearby molecular cloud origin and possible external heating from a supernova remnant. Introduction: This paper presents the first unambiguous discovery of a runaway T-Tauri star (RATTS) with a massive, extended bow shock wake of at least 10 pc, reviving the RATTS paradigm and offering a new pathway to study protostellar mass loss and large-scale turbulence in the interstellar medium. Stellar run-away velocity: UJT-1 is a strong candidate for a runaway star with a peculiar velocity of 15–80 km s⁻¹ (median ~45 km s⁻¹) relative to the local standard of rest, consistent with its observed trail and position angle, even within distance uncertainties. Appendix A Origin of UJT-1: The natal origin of the runaway protostar UJT-1 is strongly suggested by the coincidence of its peculiar velocity and position with a molecular cloud at ~4 kpc, showing matching kinematics and spatial alignment, with supporting evidence from HI and radio observations. Spectral energy distribution of the star: The spectral energy distribution of UJT-1, dominated by contributions from a central protostar and ambient dust, supports a late-type T-Tauri star interpretation with a mass of a few solar masses, as inferred from its X-ray luminosity and multiwavelength observations. Spectral type and distance determination: The optical spectrum of UJT-1 indicates a late spectral type (G5 III) and a distance of approximately 4.5 kpc with high interstellar extinction, suggesting it lies in the outskirts of the Cygnus X region. Appendix B Calculating the cooling timescales: This section derives the cooling timescales for shocked wind and ISM gas under collisional ionization equilibrium, expressing them in terms of pre-shock conditions using a piecewise power-law cooling function calibrated to solar abundances and a reference temperature of 3300 K. Wake growth scaling determination and period search: The wake of the runaway T-Tauri star UJT-1 grows with a power-law scaling of $2R \\propto (z/z_0)^{0.33}$, with a high fit quality and a possible periodic shedding frequency of $f \\sim 10^{-12}$ Hz. Appendix C Calculating the instability growth timescale: This section analyzes various instability growth timescales in stellar wind-ISM interactions, showing that only small-scale perturbations near the apex—such as those with wavelengths comparable to the layer width $h_0$—can grow sufficiently due to KH, RT, NTS, or TA instabilities, while larger-scale instabilities like KH are suppressed and cannot initiate growth at $\\lambda \\sim R_0$. Serendipitous discovery and initial observational follow-up: A serendipitous detection of a variable, faint star near a bow shock in BD+43°3654 led to extensive historical and modern astrometric and photometric observations, revealing a proper motion of −1±1 mas yr⁻¹ in α cos δ and −6±1 mas yr⁻¹ in δ, consistent with a runaway T-Tauri star. Spectral energy distribution of the bow-shock tail and dust properties: The spectral energy distribution of the bow-shock tail reveals a consistent dust emission profile, indicating a total dust mass of ~1.7 M⊙ (or ~0.34 M⊙ at 2 kpc) and an infrared luminosity of ~5.4×10³⁷ erg s⁻¹, with a possible excess in the 5–20 μm range suggesting additional warm dust not accounted for by standard models. Appendix D Vortex shedding as a possible cause of undulations in the wake: Vortex shedding in the wake of a sphere at astrophysical scales yields a Reynolds number consistent with hypersonic conditions and a Strouhal number near 0.45, suggesting it could plausibly cause observed undulations.

Our main result is the discovery of a low-mass young stellar object that fits the concept of a runaway T-Tauri star that was hypothesized several decades ago.
title: A runaway T-Tauri star leaving an extended trail
